We examined gaze perception in a sample of 111 adolescents and young adults (ages 14-30) with varying levels of social dysfunction and enriched for psychosis proneness, social anxiety, and autistic traits. We used drift diffusion models (DDM) to characterize key processes driving perceptual judgements in a self-directed gaze discrimination task, including the efficiency of evidence accumulation. We tested whether evidence accumulation was associated with social functioning, social cognition, and these different psychopathology dimensions.

Results. Impaired evidence accumulation during gaze processing showed strong associations with impaired social cognition and modest associations with diminished social functioning and elevated psychosis and social anxiety symptoms. However, evidence accumulation sensitivity was not associated with autism traits.

Conclusions. Evidence accumulation for subtle social cues relates to social cognition—a key determinant of social functioning. It may also be relevant to real-world social functioning and multiple psychopathology dimensions, including psychosis and social anxiety. Therefore, evidence accumulation should be investigated as a mechanism supporting social difficulties and related psychopathology.

articles.title: Why Produce Garden-Path Sentences? Evidence from Corpus Data across Registers
articles.authors: Rei Emura; Stefan L. Frank; Saku Sugawara; Masatoshi Koizumi
articles.abstract: Garden-path sentences induce processing difficulty by requiring readers to revise an initial syntactic analysis (e.g., The policeman saw the lights were off). While garden-path comprehension has been widely studied, far less is known about why such sentences are produced in natural language. We thus address this gap by analyzing large-scale written-text corpora in English and Japanese across five registers. English Noun/Sentence garden-path constructions, arising from optional complementizer omission, were frequent in blogs and newspaper, suggesting that their emphasis on communicative efficiency promotes their use. Rather, Japanese Main Clause/Relative Clause garden-path constructions, which arise from grammatical constraints, were most frequent in legal texts, reflecting the register's tendency to favor complexity within a clause. Legal texts also exhibit the longest ambiguous regions across constructions, which can increase reanalysis cost. These findings suggest that grammatical properties and register-specific writing styles jointly shape the production of garden-path sentences in real-world language.

articles.title: The Nature and Nurture of Visual Search: Genetic Predispositions, Experiential Shaping, and the Catalyst of Artificial Intelligence
articles.authors: Emre Orun
articles.abstract:
Draft version, [30/04/26]. This paper has not been peer reviewed.

Visual search is a facet of visual perception that utilises several cognitive functions, such as memory, attention, and inhibitory control. It is a fundamental ability present from birth, fostered through experience, and pivotal for healthy cognitive functioning. While these aspects are well established, the exact extent of “nature” versus “nurture” in visual search performance remains highly debatable. This review comprehensively examines biological predispositions against experiential factors to evaluate their relative contributions. The scope encompasses prenatal and biological factors, such as heritability and neurobiology - representing 'nature', alongside postnatal factors, such as training and societal exposure - representing 'nurture’. Furthermore, this paper utilises recent advancements in artificial intelligence as digital proxies to test these perspectives. Overall, the literature suggests that visual search is fundamentally built by 'nature' (which establishes biological constraints) and optimised by 'nurture', with deep learning models providing a novel framework to compare the indispensable contributions of both.

articles.title: Asian American Mythology and Pathways to Solidarity
articles.authors: Aeroelay Chyei Vinluan; Caroline Cha; Daniel James Sanji; Michael W. Kraus
articles.abstract: Asian Americans in the U.S. are often considered the ‘model’ minority due to their perceived success compared to other racial minority groups. However, this mythology creates barriers for Asian Americans in fostering solidarity with other racially marginalized groups (i.e., intra-minority solidarity) and with Asian Americans of different ethnic backgrounds (i.e., inter-ethnic solidarity). One barrier is the presumption that Asian Americans do not experience racial discrimination, suggesting racism no longer exists. Another is the erasure of the ethnic diversity of Asian Americans, ignoring the experiences of some ethnic subgroups. We argue that raising awareness about Asian Americans’ historical and contemporary experiences that contradict the model minority myth can overcome these barriers. Learning that Asian Americans have and currently experience racism can promote intra-minority solidarity. Highlighting ethnic variation in immigration patterns and experiences can promote inter-ethnic solidarity. Critically, myth-busting of this kind can create collective action movements across U.S. racial groups.

articles.title: Non-conscious knowledge of complex regularities supports instrumental conditioning: A registered report
articles.authors: Răzvan Jurchiș; Andrei Preda; Andrei Razvan Costea; Lina Skora
articles.abstract: Instrumental conditioning (IC) consists in actively learning to approach rewarding stimuli and to avoid punishing stimuli. This central process for adaptation requires a relatively high computational capacity because it entails the integration of knowledge regarding stimuli, behaviours, and outcomes. Recent studies using subliminal exposure show that IC occurs only for consciously perceived stimuli, suggesting that IC may require awareness. In the present study, we investigated whether IC could occur when implicit processing is stimulated not by subliminal exposure but by employing predictive regularities that are complex and difficult to detect consciously. In a novel IC task based on the artificial grammar learning task, participants gave approach (Go) or avoidance responses (No-Go) to strings from two grammars. Unbeknown to them, one of the grammars was reward-predictive and the other was punishment-predictive. Trial-by-trial measures assessed participants’ awareness of the grammar structures and of their judgments regarding the predictive value of the strings. In both an IC phase and a delayed responding phase, we found that participants responded adaptively when they were aware of the regularities and, also, when they were not. Importantly, we found reliable evidence that non-conscious regularities produced adaptive responding only when they produced conscious judgments regarding the predictive values of the stimuli. When judgment knowledge was unconscious, data were insensitive or more consistent with chance performance. This suggests that IC may be produced by a combination of unconscious learning and conscious integration.

articles.title: Testing the Relationship between Phenomenological Control related to Illusion Sensitivity
articles.authors: Dominique Makowski; Ana Neves
articles.abstract: Visual illusions highlight how easily our conscious experience can be altered with respect to perceptual reality. Despite sharing in-principle mechanisms with phenomenological control, i.e., the ability to alter our perceptual experience to match task demands or expectations, research tying the two remains scarce. This study aims to replicate and expand Lush et al. (2022) reporting an absence of correlation between phenomenological control (measured using the Phenomenological Control Scale) and illusion sensitivity to different illusion types. [N participants were recruited in an online study. Results will be added in the final version of the manuscript].

articles.title: Do young children spontaneously use proactive strategies in working memory?
articles.authors: Mathieu Zaugg; Naomi Langerock; Dominic Guitard; Virginie Mossière; Popescu David; Evie Vergauwe
articles.abstract: Proactivity is the capacity to anticipate, and to mentally and behaviorally prepare for, upcoming cognitive demands. Understanding its emergence is key to understanding children’s cognitive functioning and development. In working memory, proactive behavior can take the form of planning response sequences for the recall of to-be-remembered lists. Chevalier et al. (2014) showed its emergence around age 7. However, a high cognitive load may have influenced their results. Therefore, we used a task closely modeled after the task used by these authors and created two additional versions with a lower cognitive load. Two groups of children (age 4-6 and 8-11) as well as adults had to remember and recall series of animals. We measured response times during recall to detect signs of proactive response planning. Our results revealed signs of proactivity in all age groups, including the youngest aged 4-6, indicating that proactivity may already be present well before age 7, at least in working memory.

articles.title: Adolescents’ experiences of social media content types and wellbeing: A photo-elicitation interview study
articles.authors: Lizzy Winstone; James Parsonage; Cassey Muir; Amie Randhawa; Lauren Cross; Georgie MacArthur; Evangelina Asiedu-Addo; Emma Garavini
articles.abstract:
Background: Research on social media and adolescent mental health has often focused on screen time, with less attention to the types of content encountered within algorithmically curated feeds. Different forms of content may have distinct implications for wellbeing, yet adolescents’ own perspectives remain underexplored.
Methods: We conducted semi-structured photo-elicitation interviews in 2025 with 27 adolescents aged 14-19 years in the United Kingdom (mean age = 17.15 years, SD = 1.49; 70% self-described female). Participants shared screenshots from their TikTok For You or Instagram Explore pages to support discussion of recommended content. Data were analysed using reflexive thematic analysis.
Results: Participants described diverse content types, including entertainment, consumerism, identity-related, mental health, news and activism, body image, and AI-generated content. Across themes, content was experienced as double-edged. Perceived impacts were shaped by recurrence of exposure and by developmental and personal contexts, including mood, age, and literacy. Repeated exposure could transform initially neutral or beneficial content into experiences of excessive scrolling, pressure, distress, or emotional burden.
Conclusions: Adolescents’ experiences of social media and their perceived implications for wellbeing appear to depend on the content repeatedly encountered and how it is interpreted in context. Interventions should move beyond screen time alone to address the quality and emotional consequences of content exposure.

Key words: Algorithmic recommendation; Adolescents; Social media content; Wellbeing; Mental health; Photo-elicitation; Qualitative research

articles.title: Single- and Multilevel Perspectives on Covariate Selection in Randomized Intervention Studies on Student Achievement
articles.authors: Sophie Elise Stallasch; Oliver Lüdtke; Cordula Artelt; Larry V. Hedges; Martin Brunner
articles.abstract: Well-chosen covariates boost the design sensitivity of individually and cluster-randomized trials. We provide guidance on covariate selection generating an extensive compilation of single- and multilevel design parameters on student achievement. Embedded in psychometric heuristics, we analyzed (a) covariate types of varying bandwidth-fidelity, namely domain-identical (IP), cross-domain (CP), and fluid intelligence (Gf) pretests, as well as sociodemographic characteristics (SC), (b) covariate combinations quantifying incremental validities of CP, Gf, and/or SC beyond IP, and (c) covariate time lags of 1–7 years, testing validity degradation in IP, CP, and Gf. Estimates from six German samples (1,868 ≤ N ≤ 10,543) covering various outcome domains across Grades 1–12 were meta-analyzed and included in precision simulations. Results varied widely by grade level, domain, and hierarchical level. In general, IP outperformed CP, which slightly outperformed Gf and SC. Benefits from coupling IP with CP, Gf, and/or SC were small. IP appeared most affected by temporal validity decay. Findings are applied in illustrative scenarios of study planning and enriched by comprehensive Online Supplemental Material.

articles.title: Quest for Significance is Linked to Power Motives: Correlational and Experimental Evidence
articles.authors: Wei Luo; Ana Guinote
articles.abstract: Drawing on dual perspectives on social hierarchies, we investigated the relationship between quest for significance and the motivation to gain power, as well as their causal links. Studies 1 and 2 found that quest for significance is positively associated with the motivation to gain power in long-term life goals, and in desired rank positions in power hierarchies. Quest for significance was also linked to greater discrepancies between current and desired power, and with dominant behavior. Studies 3 and 4 investigated the causal effects of quest for significance on power motives. Situationally induced significance loss (vs. high significance) increased the motivation to prevail in interpersonal interactions, a marker of exerting power, but did not affect long-term power goals nor desired societal power rank. Together, these findings suggest that when social worth is threatened and upward mobility within status hierarchies is difficult, individuals may pursue power as an alternative route to ascend in social hierarchies.

articles.title: Reduplication Creates Order Effects in Networked Communication: Implications for Countering Misinformation
articles.authors: David J. Young; Ulrike Hahn
articles.abstract: We use simulations to demonstrate that communication networks create order effects which mean that pre-bunking is likely to be a more effective counter-misinformation strategy than debunking at the system level even if they are both equally efficacious at the individual level. The order effect is greater to the extent that it is possible for reduplication to occur, where agents integrate multiple copies of the same information packet into their beliefs, and in sparse communication networks. However, the advantage for pre-bunking persists even when reduplication levels are low.

articles.title: Third-party punishment is common across human societies but varies with socioecology
articles.authors: Zachary H Garfield; Grégory Fiorio; Ana Clara Kaneko Ebert; Sophia Iben El Rhazi; Francesca De Petrillo; Catherine Molho
articles.abstract: Punishment systems are central to maintaining human cooperation, yet how they vary across societies remains poorly understood. Different theoretical frameworks make distinct predictions about who third-party punishment (3PP) defends and the socioecological conditions under which it occurs. We test such predictions using a new cross-cultural dataset of 3961 ethnographic texts (coded 11893 times) from 60 largely nonindustrial societies. We code evidence for second- and third-party punishment and, when third-party punishment is documented, whether it is enacted on behalf of kin, social partners, or the broader community. Using Bayesian multilevel regression models, we test preregistered hypotheses linking these different forms of punishment to society-level measures of complexity (i.e., technological and social differentiation, resource-use intensification, community size) and kinship intensity. We find that third-party punishment is widespread across societies and ethnographic texts, occurring more frequently than second-party punishment. Aggregated across relational subtypes, third-party punishment shows weak associations with socioecology; disaggregated, it shows distinct and theoretically meaningful patterns. Generalized 3PP on behalf of community members is positively associated with technological and social differentiation, while kin-directed 3PP is positively associated with kinship intensity and negatively associated with technological and social differentiation. Contrary to our prediction, partner-directed 3PP shows a robust negative association with resource-use intensification. Second-party punishment is negatively associated with technological and social differentiation. These findings show that socioecological structures shape not only the presence of punishment but also the forms it takes and whom it is used to defend, revealing distinct punishment regimes rather than a single enforcement continuum.

articles.title: Key Processes Supporting Engagement and Impact in a School-Based, Indicated Preventative Mental Health Programme: A Qualitative Study of Young People’s Perspectives
articles.authors: Laura Lucas; Olivia Stirling; Alex Lloyd; Kathleen Richards; Mya Richards; Essi Viding; Pasco Fearon; Nick Midgley
articles.abstract:
Background
Increasing evidence suggests that embedding accessible, preventative mental health support within schools can play a key role in addressing rising rates of mental health difficulties in adolescence. Understanding young people’s perspectives is essential to examine whether these new approaches are acceptable and engaging, and to understand conditions that limit or contribute to effective delivery.
Methods
Semi-structured interviews (n=87) were conducted with students who participated in a randomised controlled trial evaluating a new targeted preventative mental health intervention for adolescents, the ReSET Programme. Interviews were digitally transcribed and then analysed using Framework Analysis.
Results
Analysis identified four sequential, interconnected processes as fundamental to engagement in the programme and experiencing benefit beyond the group: 1) Creating a psychologically safe, youth-owned space, 2) Building peer connection to enhance engagement, 3) Learning together through practical activities, and 4) Establishing confidence and applying competencies. 
Conclusions
These findings have practical implications for delivery of ReSET and offer valuable evidence to support the wider integration of preventative, group-based mental health provision in schools. The perspectives of young people offer critical guidance on the conditions that facilitate or limit engagement and impact, underscoring the value of embedding their insights to enhance programme relevance and implementation.

articles.title: Detection of Extraneous Visual Signals is Not Affected by the Syntactic Structure of German Sign Language (DGS)
articles.authors: Patrick C. Trettenbrein; Matteo Maran; Jan Pohl; Thomas A. Finkbeiner; Emiliano Zaccarella; Angela D. Friederici; Markus Steinbach; Nina-Kristin Meister
articles.abstract: Sentences are not just mere strings of words or signs but manifest a complex internal structure. Linguistic research has demonstrated that sign languages and spoken languages both exhibit hierarchical constituent structure which determines how individual elements in a sentence relate to each other. Here, we report the first adaptation of the psycholinguistic “click” paradigm, which aims to demonstrate an impact of hierarchical constituent structure on cognitive processing load during auditory language processing, to the visuo-spatial modality of sign languages. We performed two independent online experiments: The main experiment with a group of 53 deaf signers using German Sign Language (DGS) as their primary means of communication and a control experiment with a group of 53 hearing non-signers. Both groups were shown videos of syntactically complex sentences in DGS. A white flash (mimicking the “click” in the auditory domain) to which participants had to respond could occur as an overlay to the video at different levels in the constituent structure. Our pre-registered inferential analyses yielded no effect for our syntactic manipulations, neither in the group of signers nor in the group of non-signers. Additional exploratory analyses suggest general effects of attention during the processing of communicative signals, as even the group of non-signers’ behaviour was influenced by non-manual cues despite their lack of knowledge of DGS. We conclude that the simultaneous and time-shifted presence of different syntax-relevant cues (i.e., hands, mouthings, and non-manuals) makes the processing of the sign stream robust against disruption by extraneous visual signals and argue that non-signers attend to some non-manual cues due to their resemblance of communicative gestures independent of language.

articles.title: On the External Validity of Single-Case Designs: A Bayesian Approach
articles.authors: Marc J Lanovaz
articles.abstract: When selecting interventions for students or clients, practitioners typically need to estimate the probability that it will lead to socially significant changes in behavior. One potential solution to address this issue involves computing the conditional probability of success given prior experience and the outcomes observed in the research literature. The purpose of the study was to use Bayesian inference, an approach that considers prior beliefs, to examine how research evidence combined with practical experience may be used to assess the external validity (i.e., generality across participants) of findings from single-case research. Our results present the mean posterior probability with the high-density intervals for practitioners with different levels of experience. Relying on more research evidence and on more practical experience to make decisions tends to improve success rate estimates. Given that research on the topic remains limited, we argue that our proposal should not be viewed as immutable, but rather as a starting point for spurring further discussion and research on the topic.

articles.title: Shared Cognitive Component of Predicting Others’ Actions and Action Outcomes as well as Inferring Emotions
articles.authors: Markus R. Tünte; Jakob Weickmann; Sebastian Wallot; Stefanie Hoehl; Oliver Genschow
articles.abstract: It is well known that individuals can predict others’ actions and their action outcomes, as well as infer their emotional states. However, little is known about whether these abilities are related. In a highly powered and preregistered study, we investigated whether the ability to predict in social settings is related across different modalities by comparing participants’ performance of predicting others’ actions and action outcomes as well as inferring emotional states. The results indicate that performance on all three tasks is moderately correlated. Principal component analyses were consistent with these results by showing that all three tasks share a common component. We also examined participants’ confidence in their judgments and found that they are unaware of how good they are in predicting others’ actions. Taken together, our findings suggest shared variance across diverse forms of social prediction, while also highlighting a disconnect between task-level confidence and objective accuracy.

articles.title: When Social Ties Are Disrupted: Connectedness, Support, and Well-Being Across 49 Countries
articles.authors: Yi-Jhen Wu; Chia-Wen Chen; Yi-jung Wu; Rui Sun
articles.abstract: Social connectedness is a fundamental predictor of psychological well-being, yet less is known about how disruptions in social relationships are linked to well-being across societies and whether received social support helps explain this association. The COVID-19 pandemic created a global context in which everyday social interactions were widely constrained, offering a unique opportunity to examine these questions. Using data from 16,685 participants across 49 countries, we investigated whether (a) received social support predicted well-being, (b) perceived changes in connectedness with family, friends, colleagues, and neighbors predicted well-being, (c) received social support mediated these associations, and (d) these relationships were consistent across countries. Multilevel and multilevel mediation analyses showed that higher received social support and increased connectedness across relationship domains were positively associated with well-being. Moreover, received social support partially mediated the association between changes in connectedness and well-being across family, friendship, workplace, and neighborhood ties. These patterns were broadly similar across countries. The findings suggest that both close and more distal social relationships contribute to psychological well-being partly because they facilitate access to support during periods of widespread disruption. More broadly, the results highlight the importance of maintaining social connection and support systems when normal patterns of interaction are constrained.

articles.title: Media Selectivity Research in Light of Self-Defeating Tendencies: Towards a Novel Understanding of Media Effects on Well-Being
articles.authors: Nicole Bizzotto; Tobias Dienlin; Silvia Knobloch-Westerwick
articles.abstract: A critical gap persists in media uses and effects research: while existing work has predominantly focused on self-serving motivations for media selection, an expanding body of evidence suggests that individuals—including those with psychological vulnerabilities—may seek out online content that aggravates, rather than alleviates, distress. Drawing on scholarship on self-defeating tendencies in offline contexts, we argue that the same process may unfold in mediated environments, especially on social media: when negative self-concepts are salient, users may gravitate toward content that validates these self-views, even at the expense of their well-being. We focus on mental health content as an illustrative case in which these dynamics become particularly visible and harmful, while arguing that similar processes apply to other domains. To guide this line of inquiry and offer a more comprehensive account of media selection processes and their implications for well-being, we extend the established Selective Exposure Self- and Affect-Management (SESAM) model by incorporating self-defeating motivations and outline a research agenda.

articles.title: Using biologically inspired visual statistics to predict texture memorability
articles.authors: Federico De Filippi; Olivier Penacchio; Akira O'Connor; Julie M Harris
articles.abstract: We might remember an image for its meaning, but purely visual features may also contribute. Although some image properties that increase visual stimulation (such as size and contrast) are known to enhance memory, the role of an image's local spatial structure remains unclear. We used a computational model of early vision to test whether the patterns of activity evoked by a texture predict how well human observers remember it. Orientation- and spatial-scale-selective receptive fields were combined with colour-opponency mechanisms to quantify population luminance and colour processing of a large set of texture images with minimal semantic content. We used the model to select stimuli that offered either high or low visual stimulation. One hundred observers rated how 'memorable' each image appeared (metamemory rating), then completed a memory test distinguishing previously seen images from novel ones (recognition). Textures evoking stronger activity were associated with higher metamemory ratings and enhanced recognition performance. However, while pattern and colour contrast statistics explained a large proportion of the variance in metamemory ratings (suggesting an influence of early visual processing), they explained less variance in recognition performance, indicating a smaller influence on recognition. These findings highlight which visual signals are likely to influence memory (and our perception of it) within a neurobiologically plausible feature space.

articles.title: Strong evidence for music to improve sleep: the impact of preference
articles.authors: Rory Kirk; George Panoutsos; Maan van de Werken; Renee Timmers
articles.abstract: Understanding the interplay between music and personal factors is important for understanding what music may support sleep and why. In a night-time study, the effect of music listening prior to sleep onset was tested, comparing the effects of two different music playlists to a control condition of no music. Four outcomes were measured: sleep quality, sleep onset latency, waking after sleep onset, and sleep efficiency. After a baseline measure, 30 participants listened to one playlist or the other or silence when going to sleep for three consecutive nights each. Participants completed a mood questionnaire before going to bed and sleep diaries on waking the next morning. A follow up survey ascertained which playlist each participant preferred. Bayesian mixed effects models for each sleep measure accounting for evening mood showed strong evidence for the playlist sampled based on subjective ratings, as opposed to musical features, to improve sleep quality compared to no music. Stronger evidence was found for an improvement in sleep quality when the music was preferred, and unlike the outright playlist comparisons preference impacted all other sleep measures as well. Feedback from participants indicated positive experiences of using music with sleep and some surprise at their responses to the different playlists. The findings indicate that music with certain properties has an outright impact on sleep quality, but preference plays a strong moderating role. Feedback from participants further suggests that until they try it, listeners may be naive to the effects different music can have on their sleep.

articles.title: Evaluating Attention to Context Using a Recognition Test After Extinction Training in a Human Predictive Learning Task
articles.authors: Jose E. Callejas-Aguilera; Sofía M. Rosas-Aguado
articles.abstract: Two experiments evaluated whether extinction experience increases attention to context and, consequently, its processing in a human predictive learning task. Both experiments compared the recognition of individual contextual elements between two groups, one received an extinction treatment, while the other did not. Experiment 1 revealed poor discrimination in both groups, regarding element recognition. Experiment 2 introduced modifications to the recognition task to enhance its sensitivity. However, the results were similar to those reported in Experiment 1. Evidence from both experiments suggest the need for further research in which contextual processing is evaluated alongside context-dependency, which could indicate global processing of context instead of individual in uncertain situations.

articles.title: Adverse Childhood Experiences and Violent Extremism: A Scoping Review of the Literature
articles.authors: Caterina Ceccarelli; Teja Pammi; Sam Hibbs; Jemima Chase; Nafees Hamid; Fiona McEwen
articles.abstract: Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) are stressful and potentially traumatic events that occur during the first two decades of life, with the potential for detrimental effects on development. ACEs are associated with a range of negative outcomes, including various violent behaviors. While there is interest in the relationship between ACEs and violent extremism (VE), it is unclear how systematically this has been studied. We conducted a scoping review of qualitative, quantitative, and mixed methods studies to synthesize the literature on ACEs and VE. We searched PsychINFO, ProQuest Social Science Premium Collection, and PubMed up to November 2025. Data extraction and quality appraisal were done following the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT). 604 records were screened. We included 27 studies that consisted of individuals who supported or were susceptible to support a range of right and left wing, Islamist, and other extremist groups or ideologies, or who showed support for political violence more generally. The most common ACEs reported across studies were related to the domain of household dysfunction, followed by maltreatment. Proposed mechanisms of actions highlight how ACEs might increase vulnerability to mental disorders and impair social-level outcomes, including general criminality and violence, which in turn may increase the risk of VE. Further research is needed to explore the complex relationship between ACEs and VE through longitudinal designs, adopting standardized tools and definitions, inclusion of appropriate control groups, and exploration of developmental pathways and causal mechanisms.

articles.title: Evaluating Local Structural-After-Measurement (LSAM) and Traditional Approaches for the Estimation of Complex Nonlinear Effects Among Latent Variables
articles.authors: Felipe Fontana Vieira; KJell Slupphaug; Yves Rosseel
articles.abstract:
Various methods exist to include quadratic or interaction terms involving latent
variables in structural equation models (SEM). Most use system-wide estimation,
where all parameters are estimated simultaneously, and have been investigated in
models with few nonlinear effects. Recently, structural-after-measurement (SAM)
approaches have been proposed, where estimation proceeds in two stages:
measurement model first, then structural model. Rosseel et al. (2025) extended local
SAM (LSAM) to handle second-order nonlinear effects among latent variables. In this
article, we present a formula for two-step standard errors (SEs) not previously available
for LSAM. We also conducted two simulation studies varying latent exogenous
predictor distributions (normal, right-skewed, uniform), reliabilities (0.4, 0.6, 0.8),
sample sizes (400, 1000), and measurement error and structural disturbance
distributions (normal, right-skewed). The first study examined a model with three
nonlinear effects. LSAM produced largely unbiased estimates with stable coverage,
SEs, and Type I error rates, though power fluctuated and performance was adversely
affected under low reliability, particularly with uniform latent exogenous distributions.
Larger sample sizes mitigated these issues. Traditional methods (LMS, QML, UPI)
showed results consistent with prior literature. The second study, excluding LMS,
tested a more complex model with eight nonlinear effects. LSAM maintained adequate
performance despite greater complexity, though similar limitations emerged and power
decreased under certain conditions. Traditional methods yielded more variable results,
with generally poorer performance relative to Simulation 1 and LSAM. Right-skewed
measurement errors affected the abovementioned results more than right-skewed
structural disturbances, particularly under low and medium reliability.

articles.title: Bridging the Divide? Reducing Hostility Without Increasing Acceptance: A Commentary on  Moritz et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Mathias Kauff; Oliver Christ; Julia C. Becker; Sarina J. Schäfer
articles.abstract: In their manuscript entitled “Bridging the divide: Using metacognitive training to reduce hostility between the political left and right” published in Political Psychology, Moritz et al. (2026) apply metacognitive training (MCT), a well-established method from clinical psychology, to reduce hostility toward political outgroup members. The authors argue that presenting counterstereotypical information about the outgroup during MCT may help mitigate political polarization. In the present commentary, we do not provide a detailed methodological critique of the study. Some methodological limitations—such as the absence of preregistration, the lack of a control group, possible demand characteristics, and the absence of evidence for long-term effects—are already acknowledged by the authors themselves. Instead, our focus is on theoretical and conceptual issues that, in our view, remain central to evaluating the proposed intervention and its implications.

articles.title: The DAWN of Well-Being: Development and Validation of Daily Assessment of Well-Being Navigation
articles.authors: Petra Hubatka; Tomáš Řiháček; Michal Čevelíček; Natálie Macošková; Adam Klocek
articles.abstract: This study presents the development and validation of the Daily Assessment of Well-Being Navigation (DAWN), a daily diary measurement instrument. A mixed-method design was employed across the two studies. With respect to Study 1, an initial item pool was designed and reviewed by two experts, followed by cognitive interviews (N = 7; 5 women) and an in-depth deductive thematic analysis. The qualitative findings led to the removal or rephrasing of items characterized by conflicting interpretations or confusion. In Study 2, the refined item set was evaluated in a daily diary study that lasted for one week (N = 75; 71% women). The psychometric results demonstrated that most of the items achieved intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) values within the recommended range of .20–.40, indicating sufficient within-person variability for daily assessment. Furthermore, the test–retest reliability reached acceptable levels for most items (rxx’ > .70). The DAWN tool provides a psychometrically robust framework for examining the dynamic interplay between well-being components and the navigation strategies that are utilized by individuals in daily life.

articles.title: A simple spatial bias modification to increase statistical power in concurrent two alternatives choice tasks
articles.authors: Benedikt Valerian Ehinger; Maximilian Dominik Bernhardt; Simon Enkel; Claudius Hilser; José P. Ossandón; Judith Schepers; Titus von der Malsburg
articles.abstract: A common task to investigate early visual attention is to provide participants with a choice between two stimuli and to measure which they fixate first. However, the sensitivity of these tasks is severely limited due to strong horizontal biases, usually to the left (at least in speakers of languages written left-to-right), ranging from ~65% up to ~90% preference to one side in the absence of other differences. Schütz 2014 extended this finding to non-horizontal stimulus arrangements, showing a bias towards ~110° upwards eye movements. Here, we replicate these findings and discuss their implications for experimental design in choice tasks. In an eye-tracking study (N=23), we (1) replicate the left-right bias in speakers of German with a ~80% left-preference, (2) replicate the vertical bias with an ~80% top-preference, and (3) find a specific diagonal placement of the stimuli in which these biases cancel out such that there is overall no spatial bias at all. This stimulus arrangement improves the dynamic range, and statistical power, when studying attentional effects. Based on a Bradley-Terry logistic regression simulation, we could gain small (~5%, at ~65% bias) to decent (~30%, at ~80% bias) to large (~300% at ~90% bias) power improvements, simply by changing the spatial arrangement of the two stimuli

articles.title: Transtheoretical components of evidence-based psychological interventions for substance use disorders
articles.authors: Chrysanthi Blithikioti; Camilla Cadorin; Marcella Lucente; Lorena Pizzocri; Giuliano Tomei; Matt Field; Patricia Logullo; Wolfgang Lutz; Toshi A. Furukawa; Ioana Cristea
articles.abstract:
Importance: Psychological interventions are a mainstay of treatment for substance use disorders (SUDs), but their ingredients remain unknown.
Objective: Identify components of evidence-based psychological interventions for a SUD class and develop a transtheoretical taxonomy.
Data sources: The National Library of Medicine via PubMed, searched on 11/01/2023 (update 04/01/2025) for network meta-analyses (NMAs) published since 11/01/2018, using keywords related to substance types, psychotherapy and NMAs.
Study selection: Evidence-based psychological interventions were identified from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) included in one large, recent NMA of stand-alone interventions targeting one type of SUD. This NMA comprised 50 RCTs comparing any structured psychosocial intervention to active control or treatment as usual for stimulant (cocaine and/or amphetamine) use disorder in adults, with 10 more RCTs identified in the search update. Two independent researchers identified all intervention arms containing structured psychological components.
Data extraction and synthesis: PRISMA 2020 reporting guidelines were followed. Two researchers retrieved complete intervention descriptions (e.g., protocols, manuals) and independently and iteratively extracted components, with multiple consensus meetings for groups of 3-5 RCTs to standardize extraction and coding decisions. Components were organized into higher-order categories with shared functional characteristics and unified definitions. The taxonomy was discussed and amended in a 2.5-days expert meeting. Inter-rater reliability (Cohen’s kappa) was estimated for a random subset of 35% of intervention arms, assessed independently by two new researchers.
Main outcome(s) and measure(s): Transtheoretical components and unique intervention profiles, defined as distinct combinations of these. 
Results: 109 psychological intervention arms yielded 1592 elements, subsequently consolidated into 25 distinct transtheoretical components. Based on this taxonomy, 109 psychological arms were condensed in 40 unique profiles. The most frequently employed components were relapse prevention (34 profiles), functional analysis (32), skill generalization (29), and therapeutic alliance (29). The least frequent components were mindfulness & meditation (3) and subpopulation issues (4). Cohen’s kappa was fair (0.2-0.40) for two components, moderate (0.41-0.6) for four, and substantial (over 0.60) for eleven (remaining components had low prevalence).
Conclusions and relevance: Recasting psychological interventions as unique combinations of transtheoretical components instead of trademarked protocols could revolutionize treatment evaluation and delivery and accelerate mechanisms of change identification.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the Socioeconomic Stress Inventory (SESI) for Mexican-Origin U.S. Adults and Turkish-Origin German Adults
articles.authors: Laura Scholaske; Ramon T. Flores; Norma Rodriguez
articles.abstract:
Objectives. People of Mexican origin in the United States and those of Turkish origin in Germany face similar socioeconomic disadvantages, which constitute a major source of chronic stress for these two minoritized groups. However, few instruments assess stress resulting from socioeconomic pressures within these groups. To address this gap, we developed the Socioeconomic Stress Inventory (SESI) for adults of Mexican origin in the U.S. and adults of Turkish origin in Germany.
Methods. We developed 84 items assessing stress in the domains of finances, education, and occupation. U.S.-Mexican adults (N = 195) and German-Turkish adults (N = 201) participated in an online survey and completed the 84 items along with measures of depression, anxiety, mental and physical health, general stress experiences, COVID-19 economic stress, and subjective and objective indicators of SES.
Results. We reduced the number of SESI items using an iterative process that considered item prevalence, stress ratings, conceptual fit, inter-item correlations, and item performance in exploratory factor analyses in the overall sample. The final 15-item solution of the SESI comprised three factors: Factor 1, Educational Barriers; Factor 2, Financial Insecurity; and Factor 3, Occupational Challenges. Our findings demonstrated internal consistency, convergent and criterion validity of the 15-item SESI in both samples as well as scalar measurement invariance between both samples.
Conclusions. The SESI is a robust, culturally-informed measure for assessing socioeconomic stress in Mexican-origin U.S. adults and Turkish-origin German adults.

articles.title: Mind wandering is not an obstacle, but a functional component of human learning
articles.authors: Peter Simor; Miha Likar; Teodóra Vékony; Gabor Csifcsak; Dezso Nemeth
articles.abstract: Mind wandering, the experience when attention drifts away from the current task and engages in spontaneous thoughts is generally labeled as a mental liability with a robust negative impact on cognitive performance. Despite its established cognitive costs, the potential benefits of mind wandering remain elusive. Nonetheless, an emerging line of studies suggest that although mind wandering impairs performance in tasks requiring goal-directed cognitive control, it seems to be positively associated with implicit probabilistic learning. Interpreting these empirical findings by the frameworks of neurocompetition and offline information processing, we propose that mind wandering is an essential component of human cognition that acts as a gatekeeper between goal-directed controlled processing and implicit, automatic learning. We propose that mind wandering involves the transient reallocation of task-relevant frontoparietal resources leading to the coordinated activity of the frontoparietal central executive and the default mode network to maintain spontaneous thoughts. The reduction of executive control relaeases implicit learning from top-down constraints and facilitates transient offline states characterized by slow waves and sharp wave ripples providing optimal conditions for the processing of previously acquired information. We argue that mind wandering as a gatekeeper between controlled and automatic processing contributed to the adaptive adjustment of behavior.

articles.title: The association between young people’s problematic digital technology use and parents’ psychological distress: A systematic review and meta-analysis
articles.authors: Toshitaka Hamamura; Kyosuke Kaneko; Taiki Oka; Daniel L. King
articles.abstract: Problematic digital technology use among young people can involve harm not only to themselves but also to their family members, particularly their parents, who may experience complex emotions. However, previous studies have focused on parents’ protective or risk factors for young people’s technology use. Evidence of the associations of problematic digital technology use with parents’ psychological distress as possible consequences remains limited. We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of previous studies to synthesise the potential associations between these factors. Eligibility criteria included reports providing an association between problematic digital technology use and parents’ psychological distress. After searching four databases, 1,884 reports were screened. Forty-two reports met the eligibility criteria, and nine of these conceptualised problematic digital technology use as the exposure and parents’ distress as the outcome. The types of problematic digital technology use that we found were gaming, social media use, or other content-general use (e.g., internet, smartphone). However, no reports along these lines were found on online gambling, shopping or sexual activities. All reports were judged to be either at high or very high risk of bias mainly due to a lack of adjustment for confounders. The synthesised raw correlation coeﬃcient was r = .29, 95% CI [.22, .35]. When semi-partial correlations were used to adjust covariates from thirty-two reports, the synthesised eﬀect size was rsp = .12, 95% CI [.08, .17]. The prediction interval ranged between –.11 and .36, suggesting a considerable degree of heterogeneity. Reports from Asia tended to have stronger associations between the factors than did those from Europe. While the overall eﬀect size was modest, certain types of problematic digital technology use in young people were found to burden the mental health of their parents. Suggestions for future research include employing longitudinal designs to further establish the directionality of the association and investigate the types of digital technology involved, especially those not yet addressed in the literature. As similar to substance use and gambling, providing support for parents, identified as aﬀecting surrounding individuals, may be beneficial for their own wellbeing, as well as those engaging in the problematic digital technology use.

articles.title: The Dutch Three-Domain Disgust Scale (TDDS): Structural Validity, Measurement Invariance, and Nomological Associations across Two Large Samples
articles.authors: Ann De Buck; Lieven Pauwels
articles.abstract: This research provides a comprehensive validation of the Dutch Three-Domain Disgust Scale (TDDS) across two large samples (Study 1: n = 991; Study 2: n = 1806). Confirmatory factor analyses supported the theorized three factor structure, pathogen, sexual, and moral disgust, with domain-specific loadings and acceptable global fit. Measurement invariance across sex was fully supported in Study 2 and approximately supported in Study 1, indicating largely comparable measurement across men and women. Women showed higher latent levels of sexual disgust, whereas sex differences in pathogen and moral disgust were smaller and sample dependent. Construct validity was supported through theoretically coherent associations: sexual disgust was robustly linked to pair bonding; moral disgust was substantially negatively associated with Low Morality; and pathogen disgust showed selective associations, including consistent positive links with Low Morality and weaker, sample dependent relations with Outgroup Avoidance. Criterion validity was partly supported: predictors of Low Morality were stable across studies, whereas predictions of Outgroup Avoidance were less consistent. Overall, the Dutch TDDS emerges as a reliable and valid instrument capturing domain specific disgust sensitivity relevant to moral, social, and intergroup processes in Dutch speaking populations.

articles.title: What Executive Function Specialists Think About Executive Function Intervention and Training
articles.authors: Paulo Guirro Laurence; Drew H Bailey
articles.abstract: Executive Function (EF) interventions are widely studied, yet evidence for durable and transferable benefits remains mixed. This exploratory study examined contemporary views among EF specialists regarding which interventions hold the greatest promise, which outcomes are most valid, and why far transfer is often weak or absent. Thirty-six EF specialists completed a brief online questionnaire combining ranking items and open-ended responses. Curriculum-based interventions were the most highly endorsed approach, followed by self-regulation strategy training and non-computerized EF training, although agreement on intervention priorities was low. Laboratory-based cognitive tasks were the most strongly endorsed outcome measure, but real-world behavioral observations were also strongly supported, indicating tension between experimental precision and ecological relevance. Researchers’ preferred criteria for evaluating success were associated with their research backgrounds and intervention preferences. Support for skill-focused approaches, particularly computerized EF training, was more closely aligned with preference for laboratory-based outcomes, whereas support for context-embedded approaches, such as curriculum-based programs, was more closely aligned with real-world behavioral measures. Qualitative analyses suggested that limited far transfer is commonly attributed to narrow and decontextualized training, weak alignment between training and outcomes, limited ecological validity, the contextual and cultural nature of EF, motivational and individual moderators, and methodological constraints. Overall, the findings suggest that disagreement in the field is not only about which interventions work, but also about what kind of evidence is taken to demonstrate that they work, pointing toward more context-embedded, developmentally sensitive, and mechanistically grounded intervention frameworks.

articles.title: Whom Do They Tell and What Do They Say? Self-Disclosure to Caring Adults and AI Among Twice-Exceptional Adolescents.
articles.authors: Enyi Jen; Yekaterina O’Neil; Ho Nam Cheung
articles.abstract: This exploratory study examines discussion topics and self-disclosure patterns among 537 twice-exceptional (2e) adolescents in the United States across both human and artificial intelligence (AI) contexts. Unlike general adolescents and high-ability learners, results show a prominent Nothing phenomenon, with 2e youth being three times more likely than general or gifted populations to report having nothing to discuss with adults (29%) or AI (36%). While participants prioritize mental health topics with caring adults, they primarily utilize AI for functional learning and personal interests. Notably, twice-exceptional middle-school-aged males emerged as the most resistant subgroup, with 46% intending to disclose to neither target. Findings suggest that while AI serves as a valuable tool for interest-based development, it does not replace the adult as a primary developmental anchor. Educators must move beyond reactive support and proactively build trusting relationships to mitigate the risks of isolation and existential meaninglessness associated with exclusive reliance on AI.

articles.title: Emotion Regulation Partially Mediates the Relation Between Stressful Life Event Exposure and Adolescent Psychopathology
articles.authors: Onyx Scheuer; KATIE SPINK; Liliana Lengua; Katherine T. Foster; Jennifer Forsyth
articles.abstract: Stressful life event exposure (SLEE) is robustly linked with adolescent psychopathology, and prior work suggests emotion regulation (ER) may mediate this relation. However, research incorporating experiences of gender diverse (GD) adolescents has been lacking, despite elevated rates of SLEE and psychopathology in GD individuals. This study examined gender and sex differences in relations between SLEE, ER, and psychopathology in the longitudinal Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. One thousand five hundred eighty-one cisgender (cis) boys, 1439 cis girls, and 169 GD adolescents reported SLEE via the Life Events Scale in early adolescence (M±SD: 11.999±0.648 years), ER difficulties via the Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale-Parent Report one year later (M±SD: 12.931±0.644 years), and internalizing and externalizing psychopathology symptoms via the Brief Problem Monitor-Youth Report an additional year later (M±SD: 14.129±0.681 years). Mediation and moderated mediation analyses were conducted using conditional process modeling. ER difficulties partially mediated relations between SLEE and psychopathology symptoms. Gender moderated relations between SLEE and internalizing symptoms, but not externalizing symptoms, such that direct relations between SLEE and internalizing symptoms were stronger for GD youth and cis girls compared to cis boys.  Moderated mediation models with gender as the moderator explained more variance in internalizing symptoms compared to models with sex as the moderator, while models with gender and sex explained similar amounts of variance in externalizing symptoms. Findings highlighted: (1) ER difficulties as a partial mediator of relations between SLEE and psychopathology, and (2) differences in direct relations between SLEE and internalizing symptoms based on gender.

articles.title: Object-mass nouns specify individuation lexically: Evidence from English and French
articles.authors: Khuyen N. Le; Alan Clinton Bale; David Barner
articles.abstract: In many languages, count nouns trigger a comparison by number (e.g., too many strings), while mass nouns typically do not (e.g., too much string). However, object-mass nouns like furniture exhibit mass syntax but often support a comparison by number (e.g., too much furniture → too many items; Barner & Snedeker, 2005). Some theories argue that object-mass nouns lexically specify individuation, making them semantically like count nouns in that they induce a comparison by number (Bale & Barner, 2009), while others propose that only count nouns force numerical comparisons, leaving object-mass nouns open to contextual shifts (Rothstein, 2017; McCawley, 1975). We evaluated these hypotheses by comparing English quantity judgments for object-mass nouns to (1) collective count nouns, and (2) French judgments for translations of object-mass nouns. In each case, we found that object-mass nouns behaved like count nouns, and were no more susceptible to contextual effects. These findings support the view that object-mass nouns and count nouns specify individuation to the same extent.

articles.title: Parents Adaptively Adjust Timing and Musical Complexity in Object-Use Demonstrations during Early Triadic Interactions
articles.authors: Ana Mendoza-García; Nicolás Alessandroni; Ana Moreno-Núñez
articles.abstract: Early musical interactions play a formative role in structuring infant-caregiver exchanges. However, much of the research on communicative musicality has focused on dyadic formats, overlooking the triadic contexts in which infants engage with adults and objects. This study investigates how the musical structuring of adult object-use demonstrations varies across infancy and how these temporal features adapt over developmental time. Drawing on longitudinal naturalistic observations, we analyzed monthly home recordings of eight adult-infant dyads in Spain, including infants aged 6–12 months (4 girls, 4 boys) and caregivers (7 women, 1 man). Demonstrations were coded for spatial proximity (distant vs. immediate) and musical complexity (three levels) and analyzed using generalized linear mixed-effects models. Results revealed differentiated developmental trajectories across levels of musical complexity. Minimally structured demonstrations (Level 1) showed declining trends in both duration and frequency as infants matured. Moderately complex demonstrations (Level 2) decreased in duration but increased in frequency. Highly structured demonstrations (Level 3) lengthened significantly with age while maintaining stable frequency. Models accounted for moderate variance (conditional R² = .188–.292), indicating meaningful developmental adaptations contingent on musical structuring. These findings highlight how adults dynamically calibrate their actions’ temporal and musical features to scaffold infants’ developing capacities within evolving triadic ecologies, offering new insight into the architecture of early social cognition.

articles.title: A Scoping Review on Conversational Memory and Characteristics of Conversations in Alzheimer’s Disease
articles.authors: Annick Tanguay; Sharice Clough; Ryan McCurdy; Victoria-Grace Padilla; Kaitlin Lord; Sarah Brown-Schmidt; Melissa Collins Duff
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Typical late-onset Alzheimer’s disease (AD) compromises episodic memory, the ability to encode new events and recollect past events. Much of the research on episodic memory in AD has relied on lab-based memory tasks (e.g., word lists, short stories). It is unclear how well these tasks characterize the impact of episodic memory impairments across different domains of everyday life, including conversational memory. The goal of the review was to establish what is known about conversational memory in AD, that is, memory for the content of conversations one overhears or in which one participates, such as utterances said and corresponding referents (e.g., “I remember discussing medical decisions with my children and I said […] and they responded […]”).

Method: In this scoping review, we followed the JBI Manual for Evidence Synthesis and PRISMA-ScR guidelines to conduct the scoping review. We retained 121 reports on conversation and 3 reports on conversational memory out of the 8,351 unique records found on PubMed, CINAHL, PsycInfo, Web of Science, Cochrane, Embase, and in relevant reviews. Included reports had to involve conversation in any format and on any topic, include an AD population, be peer-reviewed, in English, and published between 1990–2022. 

Results: None of the studies investigated memory for spontaneous conversation. Although most studies on conversation did report on key characteristics of the interactional context (e.g., level of structure, number and category of conversational partners), studies also left several important details unspecified, such as hearing/vision (omitted in 67% studies) and diagnostic process (omitted in 33% studies). Studies described a broad range of behaviors during conversation, with most concerning verbal behaviors (e.g., repetitions, dysfluency, ambiguity) and only 29% non-verbal behaviors (e.g., facial expression, head and hand gestures, eye gaze). Given the rarity of studies on conversational memory, we primarily summarized existing knowledge about conversation and methodological considerations to inspire hypotheses for future research on conversational memory in AD and to illuminate decisions regarding study design. 

Conclusions: This review revealed a wide gap in knowledge on conversational memory in AD and offers a path to accelerating research on the topic. Conversational memory may be an important factor in promoting independence, participation in healthcare, and social well-being.

articles.title: A beginner’s guide to working memory
articles.authors: Anastasia Kiyonaga; Mark D'Esposito
articles.abstract: Working memory describes the temporary storage of information ‘in mind’ to guide behavior. This important ability is supported by several component processes and emerges from numerous neural and physiological mechanisms that likely operate in parallel. Here we will provide an overview of the approaches that are used to study working memory, and we will introduce the major cognitive, quantitative, and neural theories of WM function. This review should provide a working understanding of the background and current state of this evolving field.

articles.title: Charting Age-Related Change in the Architecture of Fluid Cognition
articles.authors: Camille Phaneuf-Hadd; Taylor Heffer; Patrick Mair; Leah Somerville
articles.abstract: While fluid cognition (FC) demonstrates marked development from childhood to adulthood, less is known about the pace of nonlinear improvements in FC processes, whether FC maturation converges across processes, and how FC architecture varies with age. Participants (N = 1049, 8-21 years, 50.52% female, 7.05% Asian, 0.29% American Indian/Alaska Native, 12.96% Black or African American, 0.19% Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, 61.11% White, 15.82% mixed race) completed FC tasks indexing inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility, processing speed, episodic memory, and working memory. Performance in all processes increased during childhood and early adolescence and grouped into Cognitive Control and Memory sub-architectures. Although this organization appeared in childhood, Cognitive Control and Memory became more distinct with age.

articles.title: The Properties of Personal Semantics
articles.authors: Annick Tanguay; Kim Thériault; Sharice Clough; Vanessa Taler; Louis Renoult; Patrick Davidson
articles.abstract: Recent cognitive neuroscience research has uncovered some similarities and some differences between General semantic memory (GS; e.g., knowledge about family in general) and Personal semantic memory (PS; e.g., knowledge about my family in particular). To better understand the representational content and cognitive processes of PS and their relation to general semantics, we adapted a staple of General semantic memory research, the Property generation task. In a first study, we randomly assigned 240 adult participants to a traditional General semantics perspective (e.g., listing the properties of families and bedrooms in general) or to a PS perspective (e.g., my family, my bedroom) in a between-subjects design. In a second, replication study, 124 participants completed the task in a within-subject design, taking each perspective for different concepts. Relative to the General semantics condition, the PS condition was associated with more features; these were more semantically distant from each other, and included adjectives more frequently and nouns less frequently. However, the two conditions had substantial (~46%) overlap in the frequency of their features. The findings contribute to the identification of the cognitive differences (e.g., semantic richness) and similarities (e.g., correspondence in semantic features) between personal and general semantic memory.

articles.title: An in-school mindfulness-based intervention for underserved youth: Exploring stress as a mediator of emotional well-being and anxiety
articles.authors: Brianna Paquette; Melanie Grace Albright; Heather Austin; Sylvie Mrug; Caroline G. Richter
articles.abstract: Youth from historically underserved backgrounds, including those from low-income households or minority groups, are more likely to experience increased stress and anxiety and lower emotional well-being. Mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) are a potentially sustainable, evidence-based method of providing mental health care within the school setting. This paper aimed to offer a foundational understanding of whether an MBI administered in school may improve anxiety and emotional well-being in underserved youth by decreasing stress. Although the intervention had no effect on students’ outcomes, findings contribute to the limited but growing literature evaluating MBIs in diverse educational contexts. Results indicated that mid-intervention stress significantly predicted post-intervention anxiety and emotional well-being. These findings suggest reducing stress may be a key target for improving mental health outcomes among students in underserved communities and highlight the need for further research to enhance the effectiveness and cultural responsiveness of school-based MBIs.

articles.title: Social discounting outpredicts temporal discounting in intergenerational judgment: A many labs investigation
articles.authors: Kyle Fiore Law; Paige Amormino; Diego Manríquez-Robles; Raja Intan Arifah Raja Reza Shah; Jonathan Bogard; Erin Michelle Buchanan; Nick Buttrick; Christopher Daryl Cameron; Monique Chambon; Kenneth Diao
articles.abstract:
Many current issues (e.g., climate change) plausibly also affect future generations, but research suggests that people are less concerned about the well-being of future humans. Future humans are temporally, but also socially distant. We tested whether intergenerational judgment tracks more closely with temporal discounting or social discounting. In a preregistered study, N=5,801 undergraduate participants from 35 universities across 12 countries completed measures of social discounting, temporal discounting, intergenerational discounting, and intergenerational concern. Lower social and temporal discounting each predicted less intergenerational discounting and greater intergenerational concern, although, social discounting emerged as the stronger predictor. Exploratory analyses showed a social-by-temporal discounting interaction for intergenerational discounting. Participants who extend strong, temporally-invariant concern to future generations also showed less discounting across intergenerational but also social and temporal domains. The overall pattern generalized across preregistered secondary discounting metrics. These findings suggest that concern for future generations reflects both intertemporal and interpersonal
valuation.

articles.title: Five Ways Big Team Science is Changing Student Research Training in the Behavioral Sciences
articles.authors: Priscilla Haney Hernandez; Ethan Zlotnik; Tomer Hazan; Flavio Azevedo; Alexa Nancy Carvajal; Mahmoud Medhat Elsherif; Biljana Gjoneska; Ellie F. Heeps; Savannah C. Lewis; Aoife O'Mahony
articles.abstract: Big Team Science (BTS) is transforming contemporary research—but its greatest impact may be on student training. Drawing on students’ first-hand experience and insights from hackathon discussions, we identify five ways these collaborations uniquely prepare the next generation of behavioral and social scientists: (1) amplifying access to networking and mentoring opportunities; (2) fostering a sense of agency and belonging by giving students greater participation in the research process; (3) including diverse participant populations, ethical review systems, research infrastructures, and cultural contexts; (4) enabling deep reflection on the conceptual and epistemological frameworks that guide scientific inquiry; and (5) accelerating open science adoption in early career stages.

articles.title: Analog Cognition and Consciousness
articles.authors: Earl K. Miller; Scott Brincat; Jefferson E Roy
articles.abstract: Cognition and consciousness may arise from bidirectional interactions between neuron spiking and large-scale brain waves. We propose that, while synapses store memories, brain waves provide an executive-like control process that organizes millions of neurons into lower-dimensional, coordinated patterns in real time. These waves can implement analog computation, generating new patterns of neural excitation according to underlying computational principles. Their top-down influences can flexibly route and organize neural signals, allowing multifunctional neurons to assume different roles depending on context.  This flexibility is the sine qua non of cognition. Goal-directed thought, action, and unified consciousness may thus emerge from organized, cortex-wide wave patterns that both reflect and transform the information expressed by spiking, thereby shaping it into organized brain states.

articles.title: People are willing to enforce inaccessible rules despite recognizing them as unfair
articles.authors: Piotr Bystranowski; Connie Pui Yee Chiu; Ana P. Gantman
articles.abstract: Legal and political theorists argue that laws must be public to guide individual behavior, and lay judgments appear to endorse this principle. Yet publicity is often reduced to mere formal promulgation rather than actual accessibility. Across three experiments, we find a dissociation between normative judgments and enforcement behavior. While participants are reluctant to ground legal responsibility on rules that are kept secret, they treat formally promulgated but practically inaccessible rules as capable of grounding responsibility. When considering an imaginary society (Experiment 1), participants judge the enforcement of such rules as relatively unfair and recognize that violators are unlikely to have known them. Yet, in an incentivized economic game (Experiments 2 and 3), participants nevertheless impose costly punishment on rule violators under these same conditions. These findings suggest that, despite normative views about how the law should operate, people in practice enforce formally promulgated rules even when they are not practically accessible, indicating that enforcement judgments track formal promulgation more closely than actual accessibility.

articles.title: Equivalence, Invariance, Fairness, and Differentiation: How to study whether Intelligence Changes as a Function of Context Variables
articles.authors: Johanna Hartung; Oliver Wilhelm
articles.abstract: Measurement invariance (MI) is examined as a foundational issue in intelligence research, focusing on the meaningful comparability of measures across contexts and time. The logic of invariance testing within latent variable models is explained, standard and advanced methods, including longitudinal, continuous-moderator, and approximate invariance approaches, are reviewed, and the interpretive limits of invariance levels are clarified. Ritualized and binary uses of MI are critiqued, as invariance is neither sufficient nor strictly necessary for valid comparisons, and non-invariance can be substantively informative rather than purely problematic. Context variables are framed as proxies for causal mechanisms, while limitations of indicator sampling and construct interpretation constrain what MI can establish, making careful theory-driven judgment essential. Overall, a pragmatic, psychologically informed use of MI is advocated to improve construct validity, fairness, and understanding of intelligence across contexts, rather than treating invariance testing as a mechanical gatekeeping exercise.

articles.title: Synergistic Effects of Future Orientation and Somatic Markers in Predicting  Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
articles.authors: Enrico Cipriani; Lorenzo Turi; Gianluca Rho; Simone Grassini; Alberto Greco; Danilo Menicucci
articles.abstract:
The Iowa Gambling Task (IGT) is widely used to study decision-making under 
uncertainty, and it is often interpreted within the framework of the Somatic Marker 
Hypothesis (SMH), according to which physiological signals guide advantageous 
choices. However, the extent to which these physiological signals interact with stable 
temporal orientations, as captured by the Consideration of Future Consequences 
(CFC) scale, remains unclear. The present study examined whether IGT 
performance is predicted by the interaction between CFC dimensions (Immediate 
and Future) and the tonic and phasic components of electrodermal activity (EDA). A 
total of 156 participants completed the IGT while EDA was continuously recorded, 
and also completed the Italian version of the CFC-14. Linear mixed-effects and 
logistic regression models were used to predict trial-by-trial scores and the 
probability of selecting advantageous decks, accounting for task progression. Tonic 
EDA emerged as a consistent positive predictor of performance and interacted 
synergistically with CFC-Future, supporting advantageous choices as the task 
progressed. In contrast, phasic anticipatory EDA showed a negative main effect on 
scores and, particularly when coupled with high CFC-Immediate or CFC-Future, 
became increasingly detrimental over time. Overall, these findings only partially 
support the classical SMH. Rather than indicating a uniformly beneficial role of 
anticipatory phasic responses, the results point to a more context-sensitive account 
in which tonic arousal appears to provide a more reliable physiological contribution to 
advantageous decision-making, whereas the influence of phasic responses depends 
on individual temporal orientation and learning dynamics.

articles.title: Inconclusive prospective associations between feelings of betrayal and symptoms of depression and post-traumatic stress disorder: A comment on Kapel Lev-ari et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Marika Melin
articles.abstract: Recently, Kapel Lev-ari et al. concluded, based on effects in cross-lagged panel models (CLPM), that betrayal is central in the development and maintenance of PTSD and depression. However, it is well known that cross-lagged effects in the CLPM may be spurious. Here, we reanalyzed data generated to resemble the data used by Kapel Lev-ari et al. We report contradicting increasing and decreasing prospective effects between sense of betrayal, PTSD, and depression, depending on the analyzed model, and statistically non-significant meta-analytic aggregations of these discrepant effects. Hence, the findings by Kapel Lev-ari et al. may have been spurious and their conclusions may be challenged. We recommend researchers to scrutinize prospective effects by fitting alternative models to data and to base conclusions on an aggregation of findings.

articles.title: Factorial Structure of Inauthentic Personality:  Theoretical Model and a Psychometric Instrument that Facilitates its Measurement.
articles.authors: Efrén Martínez; Leonardo Garcia; Maria Juliana Montoya
articles.abstract:
In existential approaches, personality is understood in terms of authenticity and inauthenticity. 
Currently, there are no instruments that measure it according to Viktor Frankl's theories Aim. 
To establish the factorial structure of the Inauthentic Personality Model and to determine the 
psychometric properties of an instrument that facilitates its measurement. Method. A 
quantitative approach was used involving Exploratory and Confirmatory Factor Analysis 
(EFA and CFA). The sample consisted of 1114 adults for the EFA, and 6707 Ibero-American 
adults for the CFA. Results. A battery with four subscales (threats, vulnerabilities, first-level 
strategies, second-level strategies) was elaborated with high variance explained by each 
subscale (> 40%) and models that present adequate goodness-of-fit indicators (CFI>.892; 
TLI>.87; NFI>.891). Reliability, overall (.96) and for each of the subscales (>.83), is 
adequate. Conclusion. The Inauthentic Personality Battery is a valid and reliable instrument 
for assessing personality based on vulnerabilities, threats, and strategies in the IberoAmerican population

articles.title: Increasing conflict between intuitions triggers deliberation
articles.authors: Daniel Spinoza-Martín; Gordon Pennycook
articles.abstract: Despite decades of research on human reasoning, a deceptively simple question remains empirically underexplored: what makes us reason? Classical Dual Process Theories (DPTs) struggle with this question because they posit an intuitive System 1 whose outputs are monitored and sometimes overridden by a deliberative System 2. This creates a theoretical paradox: System 2 would need to both detect the need for additional processing and perform that processing, behaving like a system that triggers itself. In contrast, “hybrid” DPTs resolve the paradoxical tension by proposing that conflict between competing (System 1 generated) intuitions serves as the cue that triggers analytic engagement. Although this view has become dominant, little empirical work has attempted to manipulate the strength differential of competing intuitions to test the core prediction that, by doing so, deliberation should be triggered. We test this prediction by modifying the classic base-rate neglect paradigm in a within-participant experiment with six counterbalanced conditions (N=263). Our design extends the classic base-rate–versus–stereotype conflict by introducing a novel source of conflict: within-stereotype conflict. We systematically vary whether the stereotypical descriptions by themselves evoke one or two competing intuitions—and manipulate whether the base-rate supports one or another stereotype-derived intuition. Increasing conflict between intuitions in this manner led to probability judgments that “neglect” the base-rate less, are less confident, and (suggesting increased deliberation) take more time to be generated. Thus, our results provide empirical support for hybrid DPTs.

articles.title: Face pareidolia in noise is not tuned to horizontal orientation energy in middle childhood
articles.authors: Benjamin Balas; Emily Westrick
articles.abstract: Face pareidolia is sensitive to image statistics. In prior work, we reported that adults’ pareidolia rates in fractal noise images depends on spectral power (steeper power spectrum coefficients reduce pareidolia) and orientation energy (horizontal or vertical orientation filtering reduces pareidolia). This tuning may reflect sensitivity to statistical regularies in face images that develops relatively slowly. For example, children only acquire an adult-like bias favoring horizontal orientation energy in face recognition tasks gradually during middle childhood. Presently, we investigated this possibility by measuring adults’ and children’s face pareidolia as a function of orientation statistics.
